During full-power operation the tank will be partially filled with sodium at $1270^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to an assumed depth of 2 in., and the upper portion of the side walls and the roof of the tank will be exposed to direct gamma radiation from the sodium (Fig.

The outer surfaces of the tank are to be surrounded by insulating material, and, if no cooling is provided for these surfaces, the metal temperature in the roof will rise to $1420^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Since the side walls are to be welded to the pressure shell, which will be at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , there will be differential thermal growth between the roof and the shell, which will give rise to a thermal stress of 80,000 psi (based on an elastic analysis) at the roof-wall intersection.
Sodium at a temperature of $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ will be taken from the pressure-shell cooling circuit and fed into a system of tubes welded to the bottom surface of the roof.
A total flow of 3 gpm will pass through this circuit at a pressure drop of 15 psi.
The coolant sodium will leave the roof circuit at $1270^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and spill into the expansion tank volume.
With this supply of coolant, the average roof temperature will be reduced to $1280^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The maximum thermal stress due to this temperature structure is 10,000 psi.

At speed levels above 1200 rpm, ingassing could not be detected with mismatching of up to $5\%$ .
Mismatching the speeds by $20\%$ produced ingassing estimated to be 0.5 vol $\%$ .
When the power to one of the hydraulically driven fuel pumps was cut off while both pumps were running uniformly at 2400 rpm, about 13 sec was required for the one pump to stop.
This one-pump-stopped condition created ingassing of roughly 2 vol $\%$ .

The sodium at and near the free surface must be cooled to below $500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to minimize the vapor pressure and hence the diffusion and deposition of sodium vapor on the components of the control-rod drive mechanism, where such deposits might create operating difficulties such as shorting of electrical circuits.
Tests have shown that sodium vapor evolution and deposition are negligible at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The lower portions of the sodium in the well will be exposed to temperatures of about $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and therefore the cooling system includes convection baffles to still the upper few inches of the sodium and a water jacket around this baffled sodium zone.

Preliminary data on the NaF- $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ and RbF- $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ systems were given in the previous report.14 Study of the LiF- $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ system was initiated recently, and thermal analysis data obtained with five compositions in this system showed that there is a eutectic containing approximately 19 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ that melts at $755\pm 5^{\circ}C.$ For the NaF- $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ system, extrapolation of thermal data obtained with mixtures containing 10, 15, and 20 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ indicates that the eutectic which melts at $725\pm 10^{\circ}C$ contains approximately 28 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{CeF}_3.$ A compound reported to be present in this system has not yet been identified.
It appears that at least two compounds are formed that possibly have the formulas $3\mathsf{RbF - CeF}_3$ and

RbF- $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ In addition to liquidus and solidus thermal effects on cooling curves, solid transitions were noted at about 425 to $450^{\circ}C$ with mixtures in the 10 to 35 mole $\% \mathrm{CeF}_3$ range and at temperatures ranging from about 500 to $580^{\circ}C$ with mixtures containing 33 to 60 mole $\% \mathrm{CeF}_3$ .
The minimum liquidus temperature of approximately $615^{\circ}C$ apparently occurs between 42.5 and 50 mole $\% \mathrm{CeF}_3$ .
The freezing point of the CsF used in the recent investigation was found to be $700 \pm 5^{\circ}C$ , which agrees with recently published values of $705^{\circ}C$ (ref.
16) and $703^{\circ}C$ (ref.
2.1.7 include some data from the earlier studies.
Therefore six additional mixtures were prepared which covered the composition range 37.5 to 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{CaF}_2$ .

Cooling curves obtained with these mixtures showed that the eutectic mixture contained approximately 48.5 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{CaF}_2$ and melted at $985 \pm 5^{\circ}C$ , as compared with the literature value of $940^{\circ}C$ .

Preliminary thermal analysis data indicate that there is a eutectic between RbF and a binary compound (probably RbF·CaF $_2$ ) that contains less than 10 mole % CaF $_2$ and has a melting point of $765^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Cooling curves with mixtures containing 10 to 45 mole % CaF $_2$ did not show reproducible liquidus thermal effects, but the thermal effect at $765^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ became less marked with increasing CaF $_2$ concentration in this range.
Petrographic examination of samples containing 10 to $20\%$ CaF $_2$ showed increasing amounts of a cubic compound having a refractive index of approximately 1.410.
A 1:1 compound (molar ratio) has been reported<sup>19</sup> to be present in the similar system KF-CaF $_2$ .

Data obtained with three mixtures in the LiF-NiF $_2$ system covering the composition range 10 to 30 mole $\%$ NiF $_2$ were given in the previous report.[20] The compound found in this system was tentatively assigned the formula $3\mathrm{LiF}\cdot \mathrm{NiF}_{2}$ , mainly on the basis of petrographic and x-ray diffraction studies of slowly cooled melts.
Well-crystallized $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ was found in slowly cooled mixtures containing 30 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ or more.

The first attempt to determine the melting point of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ was unsuccessful, but the melting point was evidently below $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the maximum temperature to which the material was heated.

Preliminary thermal analysis data and the results of studies of a few slowly cooled mixtures in the $\mathsf{UF}_4\text{-}\mathsf{UO}_2$ system were reported previously.21 Thermal analysis data obtained during this quarter were not very satisfactory because of the previously mentioned undercooling difficulty and a container problem.

The use of graphite containers for the $\mathsf{UF}_4\text{-}\mathsf{UO}_2$ mixtures was abandoned when it was discovered that mixtures containing 4 wt $\%$ $\mathsf{UO}_2$ or more penetrated both ordinary (C-18) and high-density graphite when heated to a maximum temperature of $1100^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .
Mixtures containing 2 wt $\%$ $\mathsf{UO}_2$ or less did not penetrate ordinary graphite to a noticeable extent when heated to the same temperature.
Heating curves and some cooling curves obtained with mixtures containing 4, 6, 8, and 10 wt $\%$ $\mathsf{UO}_2$ show evidence of a eutectic which melts at approximately $910^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

Extrapolation of available liquidus temperature data for these mixtures involves considerable uncertainty, but it appears that the eutectic probably contains 9 to $11\% \mathsf{UO}_2$ (10.3 to 12.6 mole $\% \mathsf{UO}_2$ ).

Cooling curves showed liquidus effects ranging from 885 to $940^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a second break at or near $845^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Confirmation of this postulate was obtained when a cooling curve obtained with oxide-free $\mathsf{ZnF}_2$ , prepared by $\mathsf{NH}_4\mathsf{F} \cdot \mathsf{HF}$ treatment of $\mathsf{ZnF}_2$ previously dried in an HF atmosphere, showed a thermal effect only at $940 \pm 5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Because of the large discrepancies between this value for the melting point of $\mathsf{ZnF}_2$ and those reported in the literature ( $872^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by Puschin and Baskow,[23] and $875 \pm 3^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by Haendler, Patterson,
and Bernard $^{24}$ , the slowly cooled $\mathsf{ZnF}_2$ was analyzed chemically and spectrographically.

Thermal analysis data obtained with mixtures formed by adding 5, 10, 15, and 20 mole % ZnO to purified $\mathsf{ZnF}_2$ show that the eutectic which melts at $850 \pm 5^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ contains approximately 21.5 mole % ZnO.

The determination<sup>3</sup> of trace amounts of tantalum in NaF-LiF-KF has been extended to include the determination of tantalum in NaF-ZrF<sub>4</sub>-UF<sub>4</sub>.
Since zirconium and uranium interfere with the determination of tantalum by the pyrogallol method,<sup>4</sup> it was necessary to develop some means of removing these interferences.
The separation of tantalum from uranium in NaF-LiF-KF-UF<sub>4</sub>, which was described previously,<sup>3</sup> was accomplished by a precipitation of the tantalum with cupferron.

According to Hillebrand et al.,<sup>5</sup> tantalum can be separated quantitatively from uranium and zirconium by precipitation with tannin in a slightly acidic solution of oxalate which is half-saturated with ammonium chloride.
Since the samples contained alkali fluorides, it was necessary to dissolve them carefully to prevent the loss of TaF<sub>5</sub> by volatilization.
This was accomplished by a careful digestion of the fluoride sample in dilute sulfuric acid in order to hydrolyze TaF<sub>5</sub> to Ta<sub>2</sub>O<sub>5</sub>.
The product of the fusion was dissolved in ammonium oxalate and then precipitated with tannin at a pH of 5 in a hot solution.
